questions.question: Ropivacaine differs from bupivacaine in that its cardiotoxic potential in pregnancy is:
questions.explanation: Ropivacaine's cardiotoxic potential is the same in pregnant vs non-pregnant females, unlike bupivacaine where cardiotoxicity increases in pregnancy.

questions.question: The concentration minimum (Cm) of local anesthetics is DECREASED by:
questions.explanation: Stimulated nerves have more activated channels, needing less concentration for block (use-dependent block). Hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, and acidic pH increase Cm.

questions.question: Toxicity of two local anesthetics used together should be considered:
questions.explanation: The toxicity of two local anesthetics is additive, not independent. Maximum safe doses should be reduced accordingly when combining agents.

questions.question: In supraclavicular block, the best way to avoid pneumothorax is:
questions.explanation: The dome of pleura is on the medial side. Keeping the needle direction lateral to subclavian artery is the best way to avoid pneumothorax.

questions.question: Lignocaine with adrenaline should NOT be used for ankle block because:
questions.explanation: Like finger/toe/penile blocks, ankle block should not use adrenaline as the structures supplied have end arteries, risking ischemia and gangrene.

questions.question: Paravertebral block can spread to which space if large volumes are used?
questions.explanation: If large volumes are used in paravertebral block, drug may spread medially along the nerve sheath into epidural space, and laterally to adjacent intercostal spaces.

questions.question: The incidence of transient neurological symptoms (TNS) with lignocaine used for spinal is:
questions.explanation: TNS incidence with lignocaine can be as high as 10-11%, which is why lignocaine is hardly used for spinal anesthesia in current practice.

questions.question: In spinal anesthesia, the factor that does NOT affect the height of block is:
questions.explanation: Contrary to general belief, rapid injection does not increase the level of block. Barbotage, coughing, straining, and addition of vasoconstrictors also don't affect height.

questions.question: Central neuraxial blocks can be safely given with platelet count above:
questions.explanation: Platelet count >80,000 is safe for CNB. 50,000-80,000 is relative contraindication. <50,000 is absolute contraindication.

questions.question: The level of block required for cesarean section is up to:
questions.explanation: Although skin incision is at T11, cesarean requires block up to T4 because intestine and peritoneum are handled during surgery.

questions.question: Elective surgery must be deferred for how long after bare metal stent placement?
questions.explanation: 6 weeks (preferably 3 months) for bare metal stent. 6 months for new-generation DES. 1 year for conventional DES.

questions.question: In left-to-right cardiac shunts, the anesthetic goal is to maintain:
questions.explanation: Low SVR (hypotension) and high PVR decreases left-to-right shunting. IPPV and N2O increase PVR while inhalational agents decrease SVR.

questions.question: The muscle relaxant of choice for shock patients is:
questions.explanation: Pancuronium stimulates the sympathetic system, making it preferred in shock and right-to-left shunts. Vecuronium is for cardiac patients needing hemodynamic stability.

questions.question: Nitrous oxide should NOT be used in emphysema because it can:
questions.explanation: N2O can expand air-filled bullae in emphysema leading to rupture and pneumothorax.

questions.question: H2 blockers (ranitidine) should be avoided as premedication in respiratory patients because:

options.B: H2 blockade leads to unopposed H1 activation and bronchospasm

questions.explanation: H2 blockade leads to unopposed H1 receptor activation, which can cause bronchospasm in susceptible patients.

questions.question: Patients on MAO inhibitors must not receive which opioid?
questions.explanation: Pethidine with MAO inhibitors can cause serotonin syndrome - a potentially fatal interaction. All other opioids are relatively safe.

questions.question: In acute spinal cord transection (first 1-3 weeks), patients are most vulnerable to:
questions.explanation: During spinal shock (first 1-3 weeks), sympathetic cut-off makes patients very vulnerable to hypotension and bradycardia.

questions.question: Alkalosis is not acceptable in hepatic patients because it:

options.B: Increases conversion of ammonium to ammonia causing encephalopathy

questions.explanation: Alkalosis increases conversion of ammonium (ionized, cannot cross BBB) to ammonia (non-ionized, crosses BBB) precipitating hepatic encephalopathy.

questions.question: The fluid of choice for renal failure patients is:
questions.explanation: Ringer lactate is now preferred over NS (NS causes hyperchloremic acidosis → hyperkalemia). Colloids (especially HES) exacerbate renal injury.

questions.question: Hypermagnesemia potentiates the action of:
questions.explanation: Hypermagnesemia potentiates the action of both depolarizing and non-depolarizing muscle relaxants.

questions.question: In diabetic patients, difficult intubation may be anticipated due to:

options.B: Stiff joint syndrome affecting atlanto-occipital and TMJ

questions.explanation: Diabetes causes stiff joint syndrome involving atlanto-occipital and temporomandibular joints, potentially making intubation difficult.

questions.question: After thyroidectomy, the most immediate life-threatening complication is:

options.C: Upper airway obstruction from expanding hematoma

questions.explanation: Expanding hematoma causing tracheal compression and upper airway obstruction requires immediate opening of sutures. Other complications develop later.

questions.question: In myasthenia gravis, the aim should be to avoid muscle relaxants by using which property of inhalational agents?
questions.explanation: Since inhalational agents have muscle relaxation property, in many MG patients it's possible to intubate and maintain surgical relaxation with inhalational agents alone.

questions.question: Shivering must be avoided in myotonic dystrophy because it can:
questions.explanation: In myotonic dystrophy, shivering can precipitate severe muscle contractures, as can succinylcholine and reversal agents.

questions.question: In G6PD deficiency, which commonly used anesthetic agents should be avoided?
questions.explanation: Isoflurane, sevoflurane, prilocaine, benzocaine, lignocaine, nitroglycerine, and diazepam can trigger hemolysis in G6PD deficiency.

questions.question: Exchange transfusion for sickle cell disease before surgery is:

options.B: No longer practiced; simple transfusion to Hct >30 is sufficient

questions.explanation: Exchange transfusion to bring HbS <50% is no longer practiced. Current approach: correct anemia with simple red cell transfusion targeting Hct >30 (Hb >10 g%).

questions.question: Carotid endarterectomy can be safely undertaken how long after stroke?
questions.explanation: After 6 weeks, reasonable recovery of autoregulation, BBB, and CO2 responsiveness occurs. Delaying beyond 6 weeks carries risk of re-infarction. Other elective surgeries should wait 9 months.

questions.question: The 'asleep-awake-asleep' technique is used for:
questions.explanation: In stereotactic surgery, patient is anesthetized for painful craniotomy, awakened for focus localization, then re-anesthetized for closure.

questions.question: In pregnancy, the dose of local anesthetic for spinal should be reduced by:
questions.explanation: Due to decreased subarachnoid space (engorged epidural veins), changed spine curvature, and increased neural sensitivity, dose is reduced by 30-40%.

questions.question: Ion trapping of local anesthetics in fetal circulation occurs because:

options.B: Fetal pH is lower causing ionization preventing return to maternal circulation
options.C: Placenta actively transports drugs to fetus

questions.explanation: LA crosses placenta in un-ionized form, becomes ionized in fetal circulation (lower pH), and cannot return to maternal circulation - this is ion trapping.

questions.question: Functional maturation of neuromuscular junction in children is not complete until:
questions.explanation: NMJ maturation is incomplete until 2 months of age, making newborns very sensitive to non-depolarizing muscle relaxants.

questions.question: For premature babies, elective surgeries should be avoided until:
questions.explanation: Premature babies are at high risk of postoperative apnea until 50 weeks post-conception age.

questions.question: The requirement of opioids in geriatric patients should be reduced by:
questions.explanation: Geriatric patients are oversensitive to opioids and benzodiazepines; both should be reduced by 50%.

questions.question: In obese patients, lipid-soluble drugs should be dosed on:
questions.explanation: Lipid-soluble drugs (IV induction agents, opioids) have increased volume of distribution in obesity and are given on actual body weight. Water-soluble drugs use ideal weight.

questions.question: Argon is considered the most ideal gas for laparoscopy because:

options.B: It is inert with no side effects

questions.explanation: Argon is an inert gas with no side effects, making it ideal. However, very high cost prevents its clinical use. CO2 is used instead due to high solubility.

questions.question: CO2 embolism during laparoscopy carries a mortality rate of:
questions.explanation: Gas embolism during laparoscopy carries 30% mortality. It occurs from accidental needle placement into a vessel during insufflation.

questions.question: In retinal detachment surgery, nitrous oxide should not be used for how many days after perfluoropropane?
questions.explanation: N2O should not be used for 5 days after air, 10 days after SF6, and 30 days after perfluoropropane as it expands the bubble.

questions.question: In post-tonsillectomy bleeding requiring re-exploration, the patient should be managed as:
questions.explanation: Patient has swallowed blood which acts as full stomach. Rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure is mandatory.

questions.question: For laryngeal laser surgery, the FiO2 should be kept below:
questions.explanation: To prevent fires during laser surgery: use low FiO2 (<30%), avoid N2O (supports fire), use laser-resistant ET tube, fill cuff with saline.

questions.question: Succinylcholine remains the relaxant of choice for trauma patients with difficult airway because:

options.B: Failed intubation and aspiration consequences are worse than hyperkalemia risk
options.C: It has no side effects in trauma

questions.explanation: Despite risks of hyperkalemia (crush injury) and raised ICP/IOP, succinylcholine is chosen because consequences of failed intubation and aspiration are devastating.

questions.question: Cement implantation syndrome during total hip replacement presents as:

options.B: Hypoxia, profound hypotension, or cardiac arrest

questions.explanation: CIS presents with hypoxia, profound hypotension, or cardiac arrest from embolization of marrow debris/fat or reaction to methylmethacrylate cement.

questions.question: For spine surgery monitoring, inhalational agent concentration should be kept at:
questions.explanation: Inhalational agents depress evoked responses (SSEP, MEP); concentration should be kept at 0.5 MAC. IV agents preserve evoked responses except slight inhibition by propofol.

questions.question: At high altitude, the potency of nitrous oxide is reduced to 50% at altitudes above:
questions.explanation: Potency is proportional to partial pressure. At >3,300 meters, N2O potency is halved. It should not be used above 2,000 meters.

questions.question: For day-care spinal anesthesia, which agent is gaining popularity for short procedures?
questions.explanation: Preservative-free chloroprocaine (short duration) is gaining popularity for day-care spinal. Lignocaine is avoided due to TNS risk.

questions.question: Able to pass urine is NOT a mandatory discharge criterion after day-care surgery EXCEPT for:
questions.explanation: Passing urine is not mandatory except for high-risk patients: urology/urogynecology, inguinal/perianal surgery, age >70, urinary dysfunction history, or bupivacaine >7 mg for spinal.

questions.question: Transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block is effective for:

options.B: Abdominal surgeries like cesarean section and hernia repair

questions.explanation: TAP block is given in the fascial plane between internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, effective for abdominal wall surgeries.

questions.question: The mainstay of treatment for CRPS (complex regional pain syndrome) is:
questions.explanation: Sympathetic blocks are the mainstay: stellate ganglion for upper limb, lumbar sympathetic for lower limb. Usually 3-7 blocks needed to break the pain cycle.

questions.question: Fentanyl transdermal patch provides analgesia for:
questions.explanation: Fentanyl patch provides analgesia for 48-72 hours. Butorphanol patch provides analgesia for 6-7 days.

questions.question: The chief advantage of volume-controlled ventilation is:

options.B: Assured delivery of preset tidal volume

questions.explanation: Volume control assures delivery of preset tidal volume (less hypoventilation) but increases barotrauma risk. Pressure control reduces barotrauma but risks hypoventilation.

questions.question: The rapid shallow breathing index (RSBI) for weaning from ventilator should be:
questions.explanation: RSBI = Respiratory rate/Tidal volume (in liters). RSBI <100 indicates readiness for weaning.

questions.question: In ARDS management, low tidal volume of 6 mL/kg has been shown to decrease mortality by:
questions.explanation: Low tidal volumes have been shown to significantly decrease overall mortality by 9% in ARDS, along with decreased morbidity, ventilator days, and ICU stay.

questions.question: The most common cause of ARDS is:
questions.explanation: Sepsis is the most common cause of ARDS, and it is the leading cause of death in ICU.

questions.question: For refractory ARDS not responding to conventional ventilation, the rescue therapy is:
questions.explanation: ECMO provides external oxygenation and gives rest to diseased lungs. Recruitment maneuvers, prone position, inhaled NO, and muscle relaxants are also reserved for refractory ARDS.

questions.question: The fluid of choice during CPR is:
questions.explanation: Normal saline is preferred. Glucose solutions cause cerebral edema. Ringer lactate contains calcium which can cause neuronal injury.

questions.question: Vasopressin has been removed from CPR guidelines as of:
questions.explanation: Vasopressin was removed from 2015 CPR guidelines. Previously it was given after 1st or 2nd dose of adrenaline.

questions.question: The dose of adrenaline by endotracheal route is how many times the IV dose?
questions.explanation: ET dose is 2.5 times IV dose, diluted in 10 mL saline (adults) or 5 mL (children).

questions.question: In newborn CPR, the compression-ventilation ratio is:
questions.explanation: Newborn ratio is 3:1 (90 compressions with 30 breaths, synchronized). For infants/children without advanced airway: 30:2 (1 rescuer) or 15:2 (2 rescuers).

questions.question: Heart rate below which value with poor perfusion indicates need for chest compressions in pediatric patients?
questions.explanation: HR <60/min with signs of poor perfusion (pallor/cyanosis) is the indication to start chest compressions in pediatric age group.

questions.question: Amiodarone dose for VF/pulseless VT during CPR is:
questions.explanation: Amiodarone first dose is 300 mg IV, subsequent doses are 150 mg. It is the drug of choice for ventricular tachycardia.

questions.question: For oxygen therapy in COPD patients, flow rate should be kept at:
questions.explanation: COPD patients survive on hypoxic drive. High flows can abolish this drive causing apnea. Keep flows at 1-2 L/min.

questions.question: The subclavian vein is the vein of choice for parenteral nutrition because:

options.B: Large diameter reduces thrombophlebitis risk from lipid emulsions

questions.explanation: Subclavian's large diameter reduces chances of thrombophlebitis from hyperosmolar lipid solutions used in TPN.

questions.question: Enteral nutrition is preferred over parenteral because of all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Enteral is preferred because: natural route, prevents GIT atrophy, less expensive, and provides more nutrients. It is LESS expensive, not more.

questions.question: In hemorrhagic shock, hypotensive resuscitation targets systolic BP of:
questions.explanation: Current guidelines favor hypotensive resuscitation (SBP 80-100, MAP 60-65) to prevent disruption of clots and reversal of compensatory vasoconstriction.

questions.question: Lactic acidosis is the most common cause of metabolic acidosis in anesthesia because of:
questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis from tissue hypoxia (shock, poor perfusion) is the most common cause of metabolic acidosis in anesthesia practice.

questions.question: Anion gap is mainly constituted by:
questions.explanation: Anion gap is mainly constituted by plasma albumin. Normal anion gap is 8-16 mM/L (average 12).

questions.question: The most reliable parameter to assess fluid status and titrate fluid therapy is:
questions.explanation: SVV (variation in stroke volume during inspiration/expiration) is considered most reliable among dynamic parameters. CVP is static and less reliable.

questions.question: Sodium bicarbonate should be given in shock ONLY when pH is less than:
questions.explanation: Metabolic acidosis in shock should be treated by improving perfusion, not by NaHCO3 (which produces CO2, worsening cellular acidosis). Use only if severe: pH <7.15.

questions.question: Tranexamic acid in hemorrhagic shock has shown survival benefit if given within:
questions.explanation: Antifibrinolytic therapy with tranexamic acid has shown survival benefit if given within 1 hour of hemorrhagic shock.

questions.question: Modified shock index (MSI) is the ratio of:

options.B: Heart rate to mean arterial pressure

questions.explanation: MSI (HR/MAP) is considered a better predictor of outcome than traditional shock index (HR/SBP) because diastolic BP also plays a role in predicting mortality.

questions.question: A patient under spinal anesthesia for cesarean section suddenly develops breathlessness, hypotension (BP 60/40), and altered consciousness. The block was given 5 minutes ago. Differential diagnosis includes high spinal vs amniotic fluid embolism. The BEST immediate differentiating feature is:
questions.explanation: Both present with hypotension and respiratory distress. DIC/coagulopathy is characteristic of amniotic fluid embolism. High spinal responds to vasopressors and IPPV; AFE has coagulopathy and cardiogenic shock with very poor prognosis.

questions.question: A 65-year-old diabetic on metformin undergoes emergency laparotomy. Postoperatively develops lactic acidosis with pH 7.08. The most likely contributing drug-related cause is:

options.B: Metformin accumulation due to perioperative renal impairment

questions.explanation: Metformin should be stopped 24-48 hours before surgery due to its long half-life. If renal function deteriorates perioperatively (common in emergency), metformin accumulates causing life-threatening lactic acidosis.

questions.question: A patient with myotonic dystrophy develops masseter spasm after attempted intubation with succinylcholine. The MOST appropriate next step is:

options.B: Abandon surgery, treat as malignant hyperthermia

questions.explanation: Masseter spasm after succinylcholine in muscular dystrophy is an early sign of MH. Succinylcholine is absolutely contraindicated (can cause hyperkalemia, rhabdomyolysis, MH). Stop triggering agents, give dantrolene.

questions.question: During spinal anesthesia for an elderly patient, the block reaches T2 instead of intended T10. BP drops to 60/30 with HR 38. After fluids and Trendelenburg, the MOST critical intervention is:
questions.explanation: This is high spinal with severe bradycardia + hypotension. Atropine for bradycardia + adrenaline as vasopressor of last resort when ephedrine/phenylephrine fail. This is approaching cardiac arrest territory.

questions.question: A 4-year-old child undergoing inguinal hernia repair under caudal block develops seizures 2 minutes after injection. This suggests:
questions.explanation: Seizures immediately after local anesthetic injection = systemic toxicity from intravascular injection. Children are more vulnerable due to increased cardiac output (faster absorption) and immature hepatic metabolism.

questions.question: A known case of aortic stenosis with gradient 65 mm Hg develops sudden hypotension during surgery. Standard vasopressors fail and patient arrests. The reason CPR is likely to fail is:

options.B: Cannot generate stroke volume through critically stenotic valve

questions.explanation: In severe AS, even during CPR the ventricle cannot push blood through the critically stenotic valve. This is why hypotension must be prevented aggressively and phenylephrine used early.

questions.question: During posterior fossa surgery, CVP catheter is placed primarily to:

options.B: Aspirate air in case of venous air embolism

questions.explanation: CVP catheter in posterior fossa surgery is placed primarily to aspirate entrained air from right atrium in case of VAE. Aspiration of gas/foamy blood is diagnostic.

questions.question: A patient with Parkinson's disease becomes rigid and unresponsive after surgery. Metoclopramide was given for nausea. The cause is:

options.B: Worsening of Parkinsonism by dopamine antagonist

questions.explanation: Metoclopramide and droperidol are dopamine antagonists and must NOT be used in Parkinson's patients. They worsen Parkinsonism by blocking already deficient dopamine pathways.

questions.question: A chronic alcoholic patient scheduled for surgery is found to have prolonged bleeding time and deranged LFTs. The muscle relaxant that would have MOST predictable duration is:
questions.explanation: Atracurium/Cis-atracurium undergo Hoffmann degradation (organ-independent), providing predictable duration regardless of hepatic dysfunction. Vecuronium, pancuronium, and rocuronium depend on hepatic metabolism.

questions.question: A known epileptic patient on phenytoin and carbamazepine is scheduled for surgery. The expected effect on non-depolarizing muscle relaxants is:
questions.explanation: Phenytoin and carbamazepine can POTENTIATE non-depolarizing muscle relaxants (they decrease the dose requirement through enzyme induction affecting NMJ). Note: The textbook states potentiation.

questions.question: A 28-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis in remission needs knee arthroscopy. The safest anesthetic choice is:
questions.explanation: Spinal can precipitate MS (direct LA exposure to sheathless cord). Peripheral nerves are not involved in MS, so peripheral blocks are safe. Epidural is acceptable but less ideal.

questions.question: Intraoperatively, a patient on aminoglycoside shows prolonged neuromuscular block. The management should be:

options.C: Ventilate until block wears off; calcium may help

questions.explanation: Aminoglycosides potentiate non-depolarizing block. Continue ventilation. Calcium ions may partially reverse the aminoglycoside-mediated block. Extra neostigmine has ceiling effect.

questions.question: During surgery for pheochromocytoma, after vein ligation the BP suddenly drops from 180/100 to 50/30. The immediate management is:

options.B: Aggressive fluid resuscitation + start DNS immediately
options.D: Stop all antihypertensives and give atropine

questions.explanation: After tumor vein ligation, catecholamine withdrawal causes profound hypotension and hypoglycemia. Dextrose-normal saline should be started immediately along with aggressive fluid resuscitation.

questions.question: A patient with Child-Pugh C cirrhosis has INR 3.2 and platelet count 45,000. The anesthetic technique should be:
questions.explanation: Coagulopathy (INR >1.5) and thrombocytopenia (<50,000) absolutely contraindicate central neuraxial blocks. GA is the only safe option.

questions.question: In a neonate with tracheoesophageal fistula, the endotracheal tube must be positioned:

options.B: Below the fistula but above the carina

questions.explanation: The tube must be below the fistula (to prevent ventilation through fistula into stomach) but above the carina (to avoid endobronchial intubation).

questions.question: A patient after carotid endarterectomy develops ipsilateral Horner syndrome, hoarseness, and difficulty swallowing. The structures likely damaged are:

options.A: Cervical sympathetic chain, recurrent laryngeal, and vagus nerves

questions.explanation: Horner (sympathetic chain), hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve), dysphagia (vagus) - all structures near the carotid bifurcation that can be damaged during CEA.

questions.question: A ventilated ARDS patient has PaO2 52 mm Hg on FiO2 1.0, PEEP 12, plateau pressure 34 cm H2O. The next step should be:
questions.explanation: With PaO2/FiO2 ratio of 52 (<100 = severe ARDS), plateau pressure already >30, and failed conventional therapy, ECMO is the rescue therapy for refractory ARDS.

questions.question: During weaning from ventilator, a COPD patient develops tachycardia and hypertension. The most likely cause is:

options.B: Inadequate ventilatory support causing CO2 retention

questions.explanation: During weaning, if respiratory support is inadequate for COPD patients, CO2 retention occurs causing sympathetic stimulation manifesting as tachycardia and hypertension.

questions.question: A patient in septic shock has MAP 55 mm Hg despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation. Noradrenaline is started but MAP remains <65. The next step is:
questions.explanation: In septic shock refractory to fluids and noradrenaline, low-dose steroids (hydrocortisone) should be started as per current sepsis guidelines.

questions.question: In adult CPR, after defibrillation the FIRST rhythm check should be done after:
questions.explanation: Immediately after shock, give 5 cycles of 30:2 (2 minutes) before checking rhythm. Heart needs ~2 minutes to generate output even if rhythm converts to sinus.

questions.question: A 6-month-old infant in cardiac arrest: the pulse should be checked at:
questions.explanation: Infant pulse check: brachial/femoral. Children: carotid. Adults: carotid/femoral.

questions.question: Emergency cesarean section during maternal cardiac arrest should be considered if fetus is greater than:
questions.explanation: Emergency LSCS should be considered if fetus >25 weeks and can be performed within 4 minutes of maternal arrest.

questions.question: Trigeminal neuralgia most commonly involves which division?
questions.explanation: Most often the pain is in maxillary division of trigeminal nerve. Drug of choice is carbamazepine; refractory cases undergo RF ablation of Gasserian ganglion.

questions.question: Conjunctival congestion is most often the first sign of successful:
questions.explanation: Signs of successful stellate ganglion block include Horner syndrome, flushing, nasal stuffiness (Guttmann sign), and conjunctival congestion (often the first sign).

questions.question: Pre-emptive analgesia means:

options.B: Nerve block given before skin incision
options.C: Analgesic given only when patient complains

questions.explanation: Pre-emptive analgesia (nerve block before incision) decreases stress response, anesthetic requirements, opioid needs, and incidence of chronic postsurgical pain.

questions.question: The calorie requirement for a 70 kg patient with major trauma/sepsis is approximately:
questions.explanation: Normal 70 kg: 1,800 kcal/day. Major surgery/trauma/sepsis: increased by 40% = 2,500 kcal/day. Burns: doubled = 3,600 kcal/day.

questions.question: Ventilator-associated pneumonia is defined as pneumonia developing after being on ventilator for more than:
questions.explanation: VAP is defined as pneumonia developing in patients on ventilators for >48 hours with no evidence of preintubation pneumonia.

questions.question: Early vs late tracheostomy has been shown to have:

options.B: No effect on incidence of VAP or ICU mortality

questions.explanation: Early (6-8 days) vs late (13-15 days) tracheostomy has no effect on incidence of VAP as well as mortality in ICU.

questions.question: Brain death declaration requires absence of spontaneous respiration even when pCO2 rises to:
questions.explanation: Brain death criteria include no spontaneous respiration even when pCO2 is allowed to rise to 60 mm Hg (the apnea test).

questions.question: The routine use of intracardiac adrenaline in CPR is contraindicated because:

options.B: It can cause significant myocardial injury

questions.explanation: Intracardiac injection offers no advantage over IV/IO route and can cause significant myocardial injury. It is contraindicated in present-day CPR.

questions.question: A witnessed cardiac arrest with VF is defibrillated at 1 minute. After 2 cycles of CPR, rhythm remains VF. After 2nd shock and adrenaline, the antiarrhythmic of choice is:
questions.explanation: After failed 2nd defibrillation + adrenaline, amiodarone 300 mg IV is given. Subsequent dose is 150 mg. Lignocaine is second-line. Atropine was removed from 2010 guidelines.
